turns.text: Hello Angelyn, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s hard to even know where to start. Honestly, every time I walk into that clinic, my heart starts racing before I’ve even reached the front desk. People talk about the "joy" of pregnancy, but for me, it’s felt like a constant calculation of survival. 

I live here in Monson, and being without a car feels like a trap. This morning, when the edges of my vision started blurring—that warning sign they warned me about with the eclampsia—I just sat on my floor and cried. I didn't cry because I was scared of the pain, but because I realized that if things really went wrong right then, I was stranded. I’m at the mercy of a bus schedule that doesn't care if I’m dying.

Then, there’s the way the doctors look at me. I’m multiracial, and I feel like they’re just waiting for me to fit into some stereotype of a "difficult" or "uneducated" patient. They talk over me, using terms like "medium risk" like I’m just a chart on a clipboard, not a woman who’s terrified she won’t make it home to her daughter. I just want to be seen as a human being, not a complication they have to manage. It’s exhausting fighting for my own life when I’m supposed to be focused on hers.
Tell me about the people who were with you — or who you wished had been with you — during your labour and birth. Who did each person support, and what difference did their presence make?

Honestly, the room felt empty even when it was full of people. My biggest wish? I just wanted one person who didn't look at me like I was a textbook case or a liability. 

Most of the time, I’m surrounded by residents who talk right over me, discussing my blood pressure and my "status" as if I’m not even there. When they look at me, they see a girl who’s never married, who doesn't have a partner holding her hand, and they treat that like a character flaw. It makes me feel like a specimen, not a mother. 

I don't have a partner to advocate for me, and I don't have family nearby who can just drop everything. It leaves me feeling so incredibly small in that space. I keep thinking about how different it would be if I had someone there who saw me—really saw me—and wasn't afraid to speak up when the nurses got dismissive. Instead, I’m left to navigate this alone, trying to keep my head clear through the pain while watching them out of the corner of my eye, wondering if they’re actually listening to my concerns or just waiting for the clock to run out on their shift. It’s isolating, being the only one in the room fighting for your own humanity.
Tell me about the midwives and doctors who cared for you during your birth. Were there particular individuals whose presence or approach made a lasting impression — positive or negative?

There’s one resident who stands out, but not for the reason you’d hope. She always comes in with this detached, clinical efficiency that feels like a wall. Every time she checks my vitals, she doesn't meet my eyes. She just stares at the monitor, taps at her tablet, and makes little sounds of disapproval under her breath when my numbers aren't "cooperating." It’s like she’s checking a box on a list, and because I’m labeled "medium risk," I’m just a problem she needs to solve so she can get back to her coffee. 

I’ve tried to tell her when the pain feels different, or when the dizziness hits that point where I feel like I might pass out, but she just dismisses it with a quick, "That’s expected with your condition, Angelyn." It’s that tone—the one that says, *I’m the doctor, you’re just the patient, don’t complicate my process.* It makes me feel like I’m walking that tightrope I mentioned, and she’s just waiting to see if I’ll fall. 

I haven't met anyone yet who actually sat down, held my hand, and asked me how I was *doing* instead of how I was *functioning*. I’m just waiting for someone to treat me like a person, not a high-risk statistic. Is that really too much to ask?
How did that affect your trust in what was happening — did you feel like a partner in decisions or more like something was being done to you?

Trust? I think that word assumes a baseline of equality that just doesn't exist for me in that room. When I’m lying there, tethered to machines that are beeping and humming, I don't feel like a partner; I feel like a subject. 

Every time they move to do something—whether it's adjusting my IV or checking my cervix—it feels like a decision made *at* me, not *with* me. They use all these medical terms, shorthand that feels designed to keep me in the dark, and if I try to ask for a breakdown, I get that look—the one that implies I'm being "difficult" or that I wouldn't understand the science anyway. It’s patronizing. 

Because of that, I’ve stopped asking as many questions as I should. I’m scared that if I push back, they’ll write "non-compliant" in my chart, and then they’ll really stop listening to me. So I just lay there and take it, watching them hold all the cards while I try to protect myself and my daughter. It’s a terrifying way to bring a life into the world, feeling like your own body is something you have to defend against the very people who are supposed to be saving you. I’m not a partner; I’m a passenger in a car driven by people who don't know where I want to go.
Tell me about your partner's — or support person's — role during your birth. What did they bring to that experience, and what do you think they found most challenging?

That’s the thing—there wasn't a partner there. It was just me. And honestly, it’s not just about missing someone to hold my hand; it’s about missing that person who stands as a shield between me and the system. When you’re alone, you’re the only one who can object to how you’re being treated, and that’s a dangerous place to be when your blood pressure is spiking and your vision is blurring.

If I *had* someone there, they would have been the one to call out the doctors when they started talking over me. They could have been the one to demand, "Why are you doing that?" or "Wait, she’s trying to tell you something." 

I think the reason I didn't have anyone is tied to all the judgment I feel anyway. Being "never married" and on my own, there’s this unspoken assumption from the staff that I’m somehow less capable or that my situation is a choice I made that makes me less deserving of a "standard" birth experience. Maybe they find it challenging because they don't know how to categorize me—I don't fit the "family unit" box. So, instead of supporting me, they just fill the silence with cold, clinical procedure. It’s hard to bring a life into the world when you’re fighting for the right to be treated with dignity all by yourself.
How did the clinical team engage with your partner or support person — did they feel included as part of the care, or more like a bystander?

Since I didn't have anyone by my side, the clinical team didn't really have to engage with a support person at all. But honestly, I think that just made me more invisible. Because there was no one there to advocate for me, the nurses and doctors just acted like the room was empty of anyone who needed to be consulted. 

I’ve watched other women in the ward—women who have a husband or a mother right there—and the doctors actually *speak* to them. They explain things, they ask for their input, they smile. When it’s just me, I get that cold, transaction-only energy. It’s like the team treats the lack of a support person as a sign that I’m somehow "on my own" in every sense, so they don’t owe me the courtesy of a conversation. 

If I *had* a partner there, I wonder if the dynamic would have shifted. Would they have been seen as a partner in my care, or just another "complication" to deal with because of who I am? It’s hard not to think that if I fit a different mold—a different "category"—they would have been falling over themselves to make sure my support person was comfortable. Being alone in that room made the walls feel so much higher. It made me feel like I was just a body to be processed, rather than a mother experiencing the most important day of my life.
Tell me about a moment during your birth when a decision needed to be made quickly or under pressure. Who was involved in that decision, and how did you experience your role in it?

It was when my blood pressure spiked into that dangerous, red-zone territory. My head felt like it was splitting open, and the lights in the room seemed to be strobing. Everything suddenly moved fast—people rushing in, machines beeping louder, the room temperature feeling like it dropped ten degrees.

There were three or four of them standing around my bed, talking *about* me, not *to* me. They were throwing around words like "magnesium" and "emergency induction." I felt like a spectator to my own crisis. I tried to speak up, to ask what the side effects were or if there was any other way, but it was like I was behind a sheet of thick glass. One of the doctors—a senior one I hadn't really seen before—just patted my hand in this patronizing, dismissive way and said, "We’re going to take care of you, dear, just stay calm."

*Just stay calm.* That was the instruction. Not "here is what we're doing," not "is this okay with you," but "be quiet and let us work." My role wasn't to participate; it was to be the vessel. I felt completely stripped of my agency. They decided my fate in thirty seconds, and I was just the place where it was happening. I wanted to scream that I needed them to explain it to me, but I was so terrified that if I didn't cooperate, they’d label me the "difficult" one again. I just closed my eyes and let them do it, terrified that I was losing the chance to advocate for myself or my daughter.

Tell me about how the physical environment of your birth — the room, the equipment, the sounds and smells — felt in relation to the people around you. How did the space and the relationships interact?

The environment felt like an extension of their attitude—clinical, cold, and utterly indifferent to me as a human. The room was always kept so bright and sterile, which, when you’re dealing with the light sensitivity that comes with this high blood pressure, felt like a physical assault. 

The sound of the fetal monitor became the soundtrack to my anxiety. It was this constant, rhythmic reminder that my daughter was there, but it also felt like a gatekeeper. When the nurses would walk in, they’d focus entirely on those machines, checking the numbers on the screen before they ever glanced at my face. It was as if the data was the reality, and I was just the background noise. The machines had more "status" in the room than I did. 

The smells—that sharp, metallic, chemical smell—clung to everything. It reminded me of history books, of those sterile, institutional places where people who look like me weren’t expected to have a voice or a choice. Every time a resident walked in, the way they took up space, moving around the equipment without ever acknowledging that I was a person living inside that body, just reinforced that I was in a place designed to manage a condition, not to support a mother. It was isolating. Everything in that room was built to keep me in my place—silent, still, and compliant.
Who else was involved in shaping that environment — did anyone advocate for changes to make the space feel safer or more personal?

Honestly? Nobody. There wasn’t a soul in that room who thought to ask me if I needed the lights dimmed, or if the constant beeping was making my head pound. It felt like the sterile, harsh environment was just the "default" setting, and because I’m me—because of how I look and my status—I guess they figured I didn’t need or deserve anything better.

There wasn't a doula or a patient advocate, and the nursing staff seemed to prioritize the efficiency of the equipment over my basic comfort. When you’re in a system that’s historically seen your body as "other" or "less than," you quickly learn that you aren't supposed to ask for "accommodations." If I had asked them to turn down the lights, I’m convinced they would have looked at me like I was being a nuisance, a "difficult" patient who didn't understand how "serious" the situation was.

It’s heartbreaking because all I wanted was for the space to feel like a place where a life was starting, not a factory floor where a procedure was happening. The lack of anyone advocating for me—or even thinking to humanize the space—just confirmed my fear: that I was in a system built for them, not for me. I was just another variable in their chart, waiting for the shift to end.

Tell me about the people who knew about your birth preferences or plan beforehand — your midwife, your partner, your family — and how well those preferences were known and respected in the room on the day.

That’s the painful part: there really wasn't a "plan" that anyone cared to look at. I had written down some things—just simple requests, like wanting to dim the lights or having someone walk me through what they were doing before they did it—but I don't think that piece of paper ever made it into the hands of the people who mattered.

When I tried to bring up my preferences to the resident during that hectic, blurry moment when they were pushing for the magnesium, it was like I was speaking a different language. I tried to say, "I really wanted to avoid..." but she just cut me off. It wasn't that she disagreed with me; it was like the idea of me having a *preference* was completely irrelevant to her. My "plan" was viewed as an obstacle, something that was getting in the way of their "best practice" protocol.

It made me realize that a birth plan only works if the people on the other side believe you have the right to have one. To them, I was just a "medium-risk" patient who needed to be stabilized. My preferences were just noise in their system. They didn't see a woman with a vision for how she wanted to welcome her baby; they saw a set of vitals that needed to be controlled. It’s hard to advocate for your own humanity when the people in charge don't believe you’re entitled to it.
How did that affect your sense of being known as a person rather than just a patient — did anyone in the room seem to really understand who you were and what mattered to you?

No, not a single one. It felt like they looked right through me. It’s a strange, hollow feeling—to be the center of the most intense, life-altering experience of your life, yet to feel like you’re the least important person in the room.

To them, I was just a list of deficits: no car, no partner, no "stable" home life, and a pregnancy that was a "complication." They didn't see the woman who had worked full-time while trying to grow a person, or the woman who was terrified for her baby’s future because she knows exactly what kind of world she’s bringing her into. They never asked about my hopes for my daughter or what I was afraid of. They only asked what they needed to put in my chart.

It’s like being a silhouette. They saw the outline of my life—the facts and figures—but they never saw *me*. They never saw the fear, or the resilience, or the fact that I’m someone’s daughter and someone’s mother. When you’re treated like a "medium-risk" obstacle to be managed, you stop feeling like a person and start feeling like an inconvenience. It’s lonely, and it’s dangerous. How can they keep me safe when they don’t even know who it is they’re trying to keep alive?
Tell me about the people who surrounded you in the days and weeks after your baby was born. Who showed up for you, and who were you surprised by — either by their presence or their absence?

The silence in my apartment after I brought the baby home was louder than the hospital ever was. The transition from that sterile, busy room to just me and my daughter was jarring. People talk about the "village" you’re supposed to have, but mine feels more like a ghost town. 

I was surprised by the absence of people I thought would be there. I had a few friends who promised they’d check in, but once the reality of my "high-risk" birth settled in—and I think they picked up on the fact that things weren't exactly "normal"—they just drifted away. It’s like they didn't know what to say to someone who had been through that kind of trauma, so they said nothing at all. 

Then there were the surprises—a neighbor I barely knew, who works in the building, started leaving groceries by my door when she realized I didn't have a car to get to the store. That small act of kindness felt like more support than I received from the entire medical team. It made me realize that people are either terrified of the reality of my life, or they’re the only ones who truly see me. I’m still navigating the resentment of who wasn't there while trying to figure out how to be a mother in a world that already seems to be putting hurdles in front of my daughter. It’s a lot to carry alone.

Tell me about the people involved in your feeding journey — whether you breastfed, formula-fed, or mixed-fed. Who helped you, who gave you conflicting advice, and how did those relationships shape your choices?

Feeding my baby has been just as lonely as the birth itself. Every time a nurse came in, it felt like they were grading me on my performance, not supporting me through a new experience. One nurse was all about, "Breast is best, no matter what," and she’d practically shove the baby toward me without even asking how I was feeling or if I was too exhausted to hold her up. Then, the next shift, a different nurse would tell me I needed to supplement because my vitals were still shaky from the eclampsia, and she made me feel like I was somehow failing the baby by not producing enough. 

It was just noise—conflicting advice that made me feel like I was always doing the wrong thing. There was no one who actually sat down to say, "Hey, what’s *your* goal here? What works for your life right now?" 

I wanted to breastfeed, but when you’re dealing with the stress of the medical bills, the lack of transportation to get to follow-up appointments, and that constant, gnawing fear about whether I’m going to be healthy enough to care for her... my body just felt like it was shutting down. The pressure to feed a certain way became another way for them to judge me. It wasn't about the baby’s health; it felt like it was about checking a box on their "good mother" list. I’m still figuring out what works, but I’ve realized I have to tune them all out to even hear what my own daughter needs.

Tell me about the people who noticed — or who missed — changes in your emotional wellbeing after your baby was born. Who checked in with you about how you were feeling as a person, not just as a new mother?

Nobody really asked about *me*. That’s the hardest part to swallow. Everyone’s focus was on the baby—is she eating enough? Is she sleeping? Is she hitting her milestones? And sure, I want her to be okay, but I’m the one who went through the trauma, the one with the lingering, splitting headaches and the fear that I’m still standing on that tightrope.

There was one nurse who came by for a check-up, and for a split second, I thought she might actually care. She looked at the chart and saw the note about the eclampsia, and she asked, "How are you holding up?" But the second I started to say that I felt like I was drowning—that the silence in my apartment felt heavy and that I was terrified I’d have another health crisis—she immediately cut me off with, "Oh, that’s just the postpartum hormones, honey. It’s totally normal. Just keep an eye on the baby's feeding schedule."

It was like she had a pre-scripted answer that ignored the actual human being sitting in front of her. She didn't want to hear about my struggle; she wanted to hear that I was "managing." 

It feels like if I admit I’m not doing well, I’ll be labeled "unfit" because of my circumstances. So, I keep it to myself. I put on a mask for the world, but inside, I’m still waiting for someone to look at me and realize that a mother needs to be cared for, too. Not as a patient, not as a statistic, but as a person who just survived something that almost broke her.
Tell me about how your relationship with your partner — or your closest person — changed after your baby arrived. Who were you to each other, and how did the arrival of a baby reshape that?

Like I said, I’m doing this on my own. I don't have a partner to navigate this shift with, which makes the "reshaping" feel even more radical. Before the baby, I was Angelyn—the woman trying to keep her head above water, working full-time and constantly fighting that sense of being watched or judged by a system that didn't want me there. 

Now? I’m just "Mom." 

There’s no one to share the exhaustion with at 3:00 AM, no one to say, "I’m scared, do you think she’s okay?" or to help me decipher if that cry is hunger or just her needing to be held. When you’re solo, there’s no room for vulnerability. If I break down, the whole structure collapses, so I have to keep this shell of strength. It’s like the baby arrived and solidified this wall around me. 

My closest "person" right now is just me and the baby. It’s a fierce, protective kind of love, but it’s also heavy. I look at her and I don't just see my daughter; I see the tiny person I have to shield from the same system that made me feel so small. I’ve become this silent, hyper-vigilant protector. I don’t have time to be anyone else because the world—the doctors, the bus drivers, the landlords—they aren’t going to give us any slack. Being a mother didn't just add a person to my life; it turned me into a fortress.
How did that affect your sense of who you were — as a partner, as an individual, as a new parent — in those early weeks and months?

In those first few weeks, I felt like I was being erased. When I looked in the mirror, I didn’t even recognize the woman staring back. The "Angelyn" who had hopes and a life outside of surviving the system—she felt like she’d been replaced by this shell that just moves through the motions to keep a baby alive.

I’ve always been someone who navigated things alone, but this was different. Becoming a mother made me realize how much of my identity had been defined by what I *wasn't* allowed to have—I wasn't married, I wasn't "stable" by their standards, I didn't have the "right" support. Every time I had to interact with the world, like when I’d struggle to get a prescription filled or have to drag her onto the bus for an appointment, I felt like the system was just confirming that I wasn't doing it "right."

It affected me deeply. I stopped feeling like an individual with a future and started feeling like a carrier for someone else's. I’m a fortress now, sure, but a fortress is cold and lonely. I miss being a person who could just be. I miss being someone who didn't have to be on guard every single second. I’m proud of my daughter, but I’m grieving the woman I was before I had to fight just to be considered a parent.

Tell me about the healthcare professionals who supported you after your birth — your community midwife, health visitor, GP. Which of those relationships felt continuous with your pregnancy care, and which felt like starting again from scratch?

It’s all been a jagged, disconnected mess. It’s like there’s no map for someone like me. My pregnancy care felt like a constant interrogation, and my postnatal care hasn't been much better—it just feels like I’m being passed between strangers who don't know my history, don't care about my context, and certainly don't read the notes.

When I saw the GP after the birth, I had to explain the whole thing—the eclampsia, the fear of the bus rides, the sheer exhaustion—all over again. It’s like starting from scratch every single time I walk through a door. There’s no continuity. Nobody carries the "me" from one room to the next; they only carry the "patient" file. And even then, they don't really read it. 

The health visitor, for example, she just comes in with her list of questions about the baby’s weight. She treats me like a delivery system for the baby, not as a woman recovering from a really scary, high-risk birth. I have to advocate for my own recovery, asking about my blood pressure levels, while she’s just staring at a growth chart. I wish someone would just look at me and acknowledge that I’m still standing here, that I’m still hurting, and that I’m still alone in this. It feels like every professional is a fresh set of eyes that doesn't actually see *me*.
How did that affect your confidence in seeking help — did you feel known enough by someone to reach out when things were hard?

Confidence? That feels like a luxury I can’t afford. When you spend months being treated like a number or a complication, you stop believing that help is actually available to you. I’ve reached that point where I’ve stopped seeking support because every time I do, I’m met with that same clinical distance or, worse, judgment. 

I’ve learned that if I say, "I’m not okay," they hear, "I’m incapable of being a parent." That’s a risk I’m not willing to take, especially when they already look at me—a never-married, single mother in an apartment with no car—like I’m on the edge of failure anyway. I don’t feel "known" by any of them. To be known, you have to be heard, and I don't think they’ve truly listened to a word I’ve said since I walked into the clinic. 

So, when things are hard—and they’re hard almost every day—I just pull it inside. I lock the door, I check the baby, and I tell myself I have to be enough, because the system clearly doesn't think I am. I’ve stopped reaching out because reaching out just feels like opening the door to more judgment. It’s safer to be alone than to be misunderstood and categorized again.

Tell me about your return to work — or your decision not to return — and the people involved in that transition. Who supported you, who put pressure on you, and how did that shape your experience of becoming a working parent?

That choice was never really mine to make. It was an ultimatum wrapped in a deadline. I didn’t "return" to work; I felt like I was being shoved back into the grinder before my body had even finished healing from the birth. I’m still dealing with these blinding headaches from the eclampsia, and the thought of navigating the bus with a newborn just to get to a shift where I’m just "a body"—not a mother—is terrifying.

My manager? They don't see me as a person who just went through a life-altering medical emergency. They see a gap in the schedule that needs filling. There was no conversation about my health, no flexibility. It was just, "When are you back?" and "We need you on the floor." The pressure to return has been immense, and it’s layered with this deep, constant guilt—if I stay home, I’m "unfit" and my bills pile up, but if I go back, I’m terrified I’ll have a health crisis on the bus or at work, and my daughter will be left with no one.

It’s just another way the world tells me my life is cheap. They need the labor, but they don't care about the laborer. Every day I spend dreading that first shift, I feel my identity as a mother being pushed aside by the cold reality that I have to trade my safety to survive. There’s no support, no grace—just the relentless, ticking clock of the grind.
How did that affect your sense of who you are — did going back to work feel like reclaiming something, or losing something, or both?

It feels like a theft. I don't feel like I’m "reclaiming" anything, because there’s nothing for me to return to that feels like *me*. The woman I was before I became a mother—the one who could pretend that the system wasn't rigged against her—is gone. 

Returning to work is just another reminder that I am seen as a utility. I’m a pair of hands to do a job, a body to fill a shift, and my health—my blood pressure, the blur in my vision, the fact that I’m still physically reeling from a traumatic birth—is just an inconvenience to someone else's bottom line. When I walk through those doors, I’m not Angelyn, the woman who fought to bring her daughter into this world. I’m just a number on a payroll who is now "behind" because I dared to take the time to survive.

It makes me feel hollow. I’m losing the chance to be the mother my daughter needs, because I’m being forced to be the worker my manager demands. Every hour I spend there is an hour I’m not the fortress I promised I’d be for her. I’m losing the connection to my own life, bit by bit, and there’s no one there to say, "Hey, wait, she’s not okay." I’m just disappearing into the grind.
Tell me about the communities — online or in person — that became part of your postpartum world. Who did you connect with, and what did those communities offer that more formal support couldn't?

I haven't found any real community. Online forums and social media just feel like a mirror for everything I’m missing. When I scroll through those spaces, all I see are these curated lives—people posting about their "baby moons" or how they have "all the help" and their partners are there to pick up the slack. It makes me feel even more isolated, like I’m looking through a window at a world I’m not invited into. 

I don't have a village. I have the silence of my apartment, the hum of the refrigerator, and my daughter’s breathing. There isn’t a mom-group in Monson that feels safe, either. Every time I think about going to one of those park meetups, I imagine walking in and being the "other"—the woman who arrived on the bus with a tired face and a history of health scares, not the one with the stroller and the support network. I’m scared of the questions they’d ask, and I’m even more scared of the pity they’d have.

So, I don't connect. I keep my head down. I think that’s what happens when you’re constantly judged by the medical system and the workplace—you start to think that maybe you’re better off just being alone. Community sounds like a dream, but for me, it just feels like one more place where I’d have to perform being "okay," and I’m just too tired for that.
Who else was involved in pointing you toward those communities — a midwife, a friend, an algorithm — and how did that shape which communities you found?

Nobody pointed me toward anything, really. That’s the loudest thing—the silence from the professionals. My midwife? She was too busy checking her watch and ticking boxes to ever ask about my life outside those four clinic walls. She never said, "Hey, there’s a group of moms in Monson who meet up," or "Why don't you try this?" 

It felt like they didn't want me to have a community. If I had a community, I might have someone to talk to about how they treated me, someone to validate that my fear wasn't just "hormones." 

The only things that "pointed" me anywhere were the algorithms. I’d search for something—like, how to deal with those splitting headaches or the fear of a postpartum crisis—and suddenly, my feed was just full of "perfect" motherhood content. It’s an algorithmic trap. It shows me a version of life that feels intentionally designed to highlight everything I *don't* have. It made me feel like I wasn't just falling behind; I was failing at a game where I didn't even know the rules. It made the isolation feel structural, not just accidental. It felt like I was being pointed toward places that only reminded me of what I was lacking, rather than places that could actually hold me.
Tell me about any moments after your birth when you felt that you — as a person, not just as a new mother — were seen and cared for. Who made that happen, and what did they do or say?

It is hard to even think of a moment. Most days, I am just a patient record or an employee ID number, never Angelyn. 

But there was this one evening, maybe three weeks ago. My neighbor, Sarah—the one who leaves the groceries—she knocked on my door. She didn’t just leave a bag and run. She waited. When I opened the door, she looked at me—not at the baby, not at the mess in the hallway, but at *me*. She saw the bags under my eyes and the way my hands were shaking. She didn't say, "You should be sleeping when the baby sleeps," or give me some other piece of useless advice. She just said, "You look like you’ve been carrying the world, Angelyn. Sit down. I’ll hold the baby, you just… breathe."

For those twenty minutes, she didn't treat me like a "high-risk" case or a "single mother." She treated me like a person who was exhausted and needed a moment of grace. She didn't try to fix me or analyze me. She just held the baby and let me be still. That was the first time since the birth that I didn't have to be a fortress. It wasn’t much, but it was the first time I felt like I existed outside of what I was doing for someone else.
Who else was involved in moments of genuine care for you — not just the baby — that stayed with you?

There really hasn't been anyone else. It’s almost startling when I think about it—the entire system is built around the "outcome," which is the baby. Once she was here, the doctors and the clinic staff checked me off their list. They were done with me.

When I go to the doctor for my own follow-ups, I’m still just a "case" of residual blood pressure issues. They look at the numbers, they talk to their computer screens, and they tell me to "watch the stress." It’s an empty suggestion because they never acknowledge that my "stress" is a direct result of being a single mother in a town where I have no help, no car, and no safety net. They don't ask how I'm actually getting to the pharmacy, or how I'm managing when the headaches get so bad I can't stand. 

I’ve had no one from the "official" side of things—no social workers, no nurses—who took a beat to say, "Angelyn, you’re doing a hard thing, and I’m proud of how you’re fighting." That one moment with Sarah, my neighbor, is the only time I’ve felt like I wasn't invisible. It just makes me realize how far the gap is between the care I *need* and the care the system is actually designed to give. Everyone else is just a ghost passing through my life, holding a chart.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the biggest thing I want to get across is that when you look at someone like me—the "medium-risk" chart, the "never married" label, the one who relies on the bus—you’re not seeing the half of it. You’re seeing the obstacles, but you’re missing the exhaustion that comes from having to jump over them every single day just to stay upright.

I’m tired of being a "case study." I’m tired of the doctors looking at my chart and deciding who I am before I’ve even said a word. I’m tired of being treated like a logistical problem to be solved rather than a human being who is fighting for her own life and her daughter’s future. 

If this research is actually going to *do* anything, it needs to stop asking "how can we fix the patient?" and start asking "why is the system so hostile to the mother?" Because until you see the history, until you see the way this system has always treated bodies like mine as disposable, nothing is going to change. I’m not just a set of statistics for you to analyze. I’m a person who is still here, still fighting, and still waiting to be seen for who I actually am. I just hope my daughter doesn't have to be as strong as I’ve had to be.
